name: Germany
consulate: U.S. Embassy Berlin / U.S. Consulate Frankfurt
metaTitle: E-2 Visa for German Nationals | Watson Immigration Law
metaDescription: Germany is the fourth-largest source of E-2 investors. Guidance on the E-2 Treaty Investor Visa for German nationals — 5-year visa validity, Berlin/Frankfurt consulate processing, manufacturing and engineering investment focus.
intro: Germany is the fourth-largest source of E-2 Treaty Investor Visa holders globally, with over 3,200 visas issued annually. German investors most commonly bring manufacturing expertise, engineering services, and technology businesses to the United States — sectors where Germany's industrial base creates natural cross-Atlantic business opportunities. Watson Immigration Law represents German investors entering the U.S. market across a wide range of business types.

sections.heading: Treaty Status & History
sections.body: Germany and the United States have maintained a Treaty of Friendship, Commerce and Navigation in force since 1956. German nationals have full, uninterrupted E-2 access and receive a 5-year visa stamp — one of the longest in the program. The German-U.S. business relationship is among the most active in the world, and E-2 is a primary vehicle for German entrepreneurs and executives seeking to establish or manage U.S. operations.

sections.heading: Common Business Types for German E-2 Investors
sections.body: German investors in the United States most commonly invest in precision manufacturing, engineering services, automotive-related businesses, technology consulting, chemical and materials companies, and professional services firms. The German Mittelstand — the country's world-renowned mid-sized manufacturing sector — is particularly active in establishing U.S. subsidiaries via E-2.

sections.heading: Consulate Processing
sections.body: German nationals apply for E-2 at the U.S. Embassy in Berlin or the U.S. Consulate General in Frankfurt. Both offices handle E-2 applications routinely. Processing times after interview are typically 4–8 weeks. Germany's business culture emphasizes thorough documentation, which aligns well with E-2's requirement for a comprehensive business plan and detailed financial projections.

sections.heading: Investment Considerations for German Investors
sections.body: German investors typically make substantial investments — often in the $200,000–$1,000,000 range — reflecting the industrial or manufacturing orientation of many German-owned U.S. enterprises. The 'substantial investment' standard is applied proportionally to the total cost of the enterprise, so a larger business will require a larger investment to satisfy the requirement.

faqs.question: Do German nationals qualify for the E-2 visa?
faqs.answer: Yes. Germany has had an E-2 treaty with the United States since 1956. German nationals are the fourth-largest group of E-2 visa holders globally, with over 3,200 visas issued annually.

faqs.question: How long does the E-2 visa last for German nationals?
faqs.answer: German nationals receive a 5-year visa stamp, with 2-year entry admissions. The visa is renewable indefinitely.

faqs.question: Can a German GmbH open a U.S. subsidiary on E-2?
faqs.answer: Yes. German nationals who own at least 50% of a U.S. entity — established as a U.S. LLC, corporation, or other structure — can apply for E-2 status to manage that entity. The investment into the U.S. company must be substantial and at risk.

faqs.question: What types of businesses are German E-2 investors most successful with?
faqs.answer: German E-2 investors most commonly succeed with manufacturing, engineering, precision technology, automotive components, specialty chemicals, and professional consulting businesses. These sectors align with Germany's industrial strengths and typically produce the kind of documented revenue projections that satisfy U.S. immigration standards.

faqs.question: Do I need to leave Germany to run my U.S. business on E-2?
faqs.answer: E-2 requires you to be coming to the United States to direct and develop the enterprise — you must actively manage the business. However, there is no requirement to permanently relocate. Many E-2 investors split their time between Germany and the U.S., with the U.S. operations managed day-to-day by authorized personnel.
